author: @lukebitton3694
text: I would've thought circular based cones would be best, but organised in a hexagonal as oppose to square based 2D lattice arrangement. This would maximise the contact area of the base with the solar cell. Although I don't believe this will give 100% coverage (thinking back to solid state physics). I think this arrangement would be most resiliant to rotation of the sun about the vertical axis, but of course it changes its elevation angle and the optimum slop angle of the cones would vary to allow the rays hit the panel parallel to its normal. The problems would arise with losses where cones effectively shadow other cones. There will be a degree of transmitted light hitting the lower portions of the structures but most would be refracted. I think just geomertrically there would be a lot of losses as well as materially. I would be surprised if it ended up more efficient. I feel like a solar tracker with an arduino and a few stepper motors... perhaps with fluorescent would be optimum, or just further research into more efficient materials.

author: @DanielSMatthews
text: Yeah played with that idea about 25 years ago, got some design files with diagrams too, and a whole lot of other neato ideas including a convection driven logic system that make a solar driven HVAC system that computes its own settings. Fluid based logic is very interesting and air is a fluid. There is more that one way to do the "prism" idea, like you say but pyramids or hexagonal forms (hard to explain without a drawing) or you can make them nanoscale and rely on diffraction over the top edge to bend the light downward and also make them serve as top conductors, graphene perhaps. And like you say you never end up with a net gain. Prisms are more useful for scenarios where you have edge exposed stacks of junctions with different band gaps, then the spectral spread can be tuned to illuminate the matched junction. I was very into this stuff until I realised that running civilisation on PV is suicidal due to the regular threat of volcanic winters of +2 years duration.

author: @humanistwriting5477
text:
I think you've done it, you've made passive solar collection as efficient and effective as active systems in what I would presume to be equal or slightly better than the same volumetric efficiency as active, and thats without looking at the backscatter gathering youd get with my back of the napkin math I just did!
And it could be much cheaper depending on the medium used for the prism.

But more then that, active solar trackers are not very human friendly in the way they use thier space, this could be very human friendly, as a steeped roof or ground installation and relatively self cleaning.
Now if I may be as bold as to suggest a glance at nonlinear optics for that prism to reduce costs and further increase efficiency? You could end up with a very low cost and low weight product quite capable of installation just about anywhere that a prism of this size would be practical
And because you can get a nifty light trap guiding all the luminosity of the reactive glow towards the collector or P.V. panel you might even have a nice way to extend the output of a solar panel for quite a few hours after dark, even if it is not anywhere near output during the day some of these compounds still act like a battery and I imagine a solid prism capable of touching as much sunlight as a active tilting tracker would be able to store quite a bit of energy if you wanted to give the trade off for peak energy output for lasting energy output.

Now I am quite curious about glow in the dark efficiencies and down the research rabbit hole I shall go! A full hypothesis shall be formed rather shortly.

author: @rattusfinkus
text: I had an idea about 15 years ago for a concentrated solar collector. Basically use an ice-cream shaped clear plastic bottle, so a hemisphere connected to a cone. Place the pv cell at the pointy end of the cone (cut the tip of the cone flat to the size of the cell). Coat the cone part of the collector with a reflective material (mirrored) and then fill the whole collector with water so the hemisphere becomes a large lens. I mocked one up with a PET soft drink bottle and silver foil and it seamed to work well. You could used PET bottle machinery to make the collector and you could place the PV cells in the caps. The water also help to conduct the heat out of the pv cell and dissipate it.

@captainnemo6655 That's interesting. I guess I tend to be biased because my work with PV cells has focused 100% on maximizing power generation and not a mixed use application like this. replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: You are actually correct. The device you describe is called a Light Trap. Whether in photosynthesis or in a photovoltaic system: if you want to use light efficiently, you have to absorb it as completely as possible. However, this is difficult if the absorption is to take place in a thin layer of material that normally lets a large part of the light pass through. Now, research teams from TU Wien and from The Hebrew University of Jerusalem have found a surprising trick that allows a beam of light to be completely absorbed even in the thinnest of layers: They built a "light trap" around the thin layer using mirrors and lenses, in which the light beam is steered in a circle and then superimposed on itself -- exactly in such a way that the beam of light blocks itself and can no longer leave the system. Thus, the light has no choice but to be absorbed by the thin layer -- there is no other way out. This is the absorption-amplification method, which you described.

author: @AnalystPrime
text:
I recall at least two projects that use something like this; one was a cone shaped solar panel so a part of it would always face the Sun and it was also spinning so while the glass on top of it concentrated light on the cells the spin constantly turned them away from the Sun so they cooled down and don't lose efficiency due to overheating, and the other was about covering walls, flat roofs and roads with solar panels.
The cones have many problems like the special shape would be far more expensive and making it spin requires a motor that takes energy, so the only part of that idea I think might actually work was using them as art and getting people used to idea of having solar panels all over the city by making them look nicer than the regular flat panels.
The other has been repeatedly "busted" by people calling it "solar freaking roadways" who keep pointing out that solar panels that are flat on the ground would not collect light well when the Sun is not directly overhead...you know, that exact thing this is supposed to fix...

replies.text: Don't give up by the likes of the "Busters" like Thunderfoot... There have been amazing breakthroughs just this year alone. Have you seen or heard the news about transparent Photovoltaic Glass and Semitransparent Graphene Photovoltaic panels? Rob has a video on how to make these. These new advances in materials are finally being put to use on buildings like the 550 Spencer in Australia. Also if you check out YouTuber Nighthawkinlight, he has an excellent video on paint that can cool down surfaces.

replies.author: @AnalystPrime
replies.text:
@captainnemo6655 Oh, right, TF "busted" the solar cones too, except instead of pointing out any of the real problems with it he focused on a claim someone made that by collecting light equally well regardless of which direction the Sun is shining and by avoiding efficiency loss from overheating they would be up to four times as effective, or something like that. Advertising hype, in other words; IRL it might mean that regular solar panels lose up to 20W of production from overheating but this one only loses 5W.

So TF spent the video explaining that overheating is not a real thing and if you use four panels you get four times efficiency, which is wrong, the panels would use 4x area to capture 4x sunlight and probably cost 4x as much, so producing four times as much power is as efficient as using a single regular panel. Frankly, it was a weird rant that made me wonder if he actually knows how solar panels work and what is the difference between panel efficiency and capacity factor...

Anyways, yes, lots of improvements have been made and some of them are even getting out of the labs and into production. Affordable and efficient enough PV glass is probably the next big thing, most buildings in sunny areas could use a little shading in the windows and with the huge area the windows add up to it is not much of a problem that the panels are less efficient.

Thin film panels may work with that too, they are so thin they let some light through so you could use them on windows without needing many extra lamps inside. Most importantly, they do not use silicon, which may be a common and pretty cheap material, but does require lots of energy and some nasty chemicals to purify it enough for use in PV cells and computer chips. Only problem is if you just glue them to the glass they would degrade quickly and need replacing often, while putting them inside the window panel would mean having to replace whole windows which is more work than regular panels attached on top of the roof or wall material.

author: @jtcustomknives
text: I think the problem is the amount of exposed area to the sun not the angle. 1 Square Foot of sunlight only has X amount of energy. If your not exactly 90° to the 1 square foot then your actually exposed to less of that area. Think of it like this. If your looking at a sheet of plywood from say 100 yards away at 90° to the face it will look like it’s 4ftx8ft. But say your buddy turns it 45° to you. Now that sheet is going to look like it’s 2ftx8ft. The steeper the angle the narrower the sheet is going to look. You would have to build a florescent triangle on top of the panel that always presented its self to the sun as being at least as large as the panels face. Think like a pyramid with the center height at the same width as the panels

replies.author: @Drjtherrien
replies.text: This is exactly the issue that solar tracking is meant for. It is true that low angles lead to increased reflection but the major loss is the total energy available for the panel to use. And another fun fact, high efficiency solar cells are textured so that there are already micron sized pyramids all over the front surface but that's done to actually make the light skim the now tilted surface to increase the effective junction thickness.

replies.author: @nigelwilliams7920
replies.text: The compounding issue is that at low sun angles, the intensity at the receiver is much lower due to passage through the often cloudy or hazy atmosphere. So the energy you are fretting about is simply not there. A flat array aimed at the noonday sun works best. Add a few more panels if you need more power.

author: @wibblywobblyidiotvision
text:
Hi Rob.  There's far more of an issue than reduction in transmission, the whole issue with loss is to do with angle of incidence of the rays on the panel as a whole, and not with the angle of impingement of the rays WRT the surface of the panel.  It's the same thing that causes seasons.  With 90°incidence, one square metre of solar rays will impinge on 1 square metre of solar panel, and you'll get high efficiency.  As the Angle goes down, so does the amount of solar power hitting the surface of the panel, relative to the cosine of the angle IIRC., It doesn't matter if you're turning those rays to hit the panel at 90°, there's still vastly less energy hitting the panel, and that's all she wrote.

This is why sun tracking is so important.  Now, most solar tracking systems rely on all sorts of computing power to work, they are expensive and "fragile" (both in terms of physical robustness and in terms of software stability).  But purely passive systems exist, these re physically and conceptually robust, require little to no fettling, and "just work" .  But you don't see them often because more computers is more better, right.  Try hitting the googles with "Wilf Rigter Power Smart Head"

author: @hlavaatch
text:
Main problem is not the direction of light (well except for extreme angles due to Fresnel reflection), but the fact that effective area from the point of sun goes down with cosine of incidece angle, lowering the amount of energy you can capture at all.
So, you would have to somehow make the prism as tall as the solar panel is wide to capture the same amount of energy from all sun directions, in addition to making the prism redirect the light towards the panel
